column10: "Page, N.D. June 10, 1945 3:30 p.m. Dear Sam, One whole week. Imagine! And itâ€™s a lovely Sunday afternoon. The first sunny day in the whole week. Page is so quiet that all you hear is birds and more birds, with an occasional squawk from the kids on the corner. Some kids! How are you? Iâ€™m fine, except when I stop to think, and then Iâ€™m lonesome. And only a week of summer gone. Tsk, tsk! You know, I wasnâ€™t going to write until Iâ€™d had a letter (one is coming, isnâ€™t it?) but we are going to Fargo for a convention early Tuesday morning and wonâ€™t get back till Friday or Saturday. If you send a letter Monday morning it wonâ€™t get here until"

column10: "Tuesday morning and weâ€™ll be gone. So, instead of me not writing for another week, here I â€œyam,â€ and hope you are the same. Oh yes, Iâ€™m on the front steps eating a choc. Hershey. Want some? Dadâ€™s in the country for services and Momâ€™s in the house visiting with an old negro lady who had dinner with us. Her mother was a slave in the south who married a half negro & came north to farm over 50 yrs. ago. Sheâ€™s very negro-like, and lovely as can be. Bernetta is still in England and says she will probably be there for another month, which suits us ok. Say, guess who I saw on Friday? Heâ€™s probably told you. Anyway I looked up with a plate of food for someone and there was Hans Froslee. You see, we were at a huge stock sale â€“ Aberdeen Angus, pure bred â€“ and the L. Aid was serving dinner so I helped. It was some sale, & I had a picnic watching it. "

column10: "Mr. Noble, the manager of the Hartley farms, sold 138 cows & 13 bulls & all averaged a little less that $800.00 a piece. The prize bull sold to a N. York man for $7500.00! I got a bang out of watching one white-haired old lady from Illinois â€“ worth 14 million â€“ sitting on the 1st bleacher bench chewing gum and bidding away like an old veteran, which she no doubt was. Mr. Froslee had a good time too, but he said he was broke so he couldnâ€™t bid, but he sold some of his cattle to a Mont. man. Heâ€™s awfully thin, isnâ€™t he? His last sale must have been hard on him. Anyway, the sale was fun, & the L. Aid made $200.00 & Mr. Noble cleared about $98,000.00. Can you imagine N.D. having a place like that? Iâ€™m glad I donâ€™t have to worry about what to do with $98,000 or 14 million. What a job! So far all Iâ€™ve done is clean. Iâ€™m nearly through housecleaning the kitchen, back porch left. This place is much too big. But I should be glad, shouldnâ€™t I, that it gives me something to do."

column10: "Oh, weâ€™ve mowed the lawn, which is terrifically tough, and pulled a few weeds. Dad has planted garden â€“ potatoes & corn! Both are doing well, thank you. Youâ€™ve been doing more than clean, no doubt! Want some help? Dad loves to milk cows but I donâ€™t know how the cows like it. He suggested that he stop off in Vining on his way home from St. Paul! Donâ€™t get worried, I donâ€™t think he will. And donâ€™t worry, anyway. Both he and Mom are very nice about a lot of things. Blow, blow! I mean, I think you & they will get on all right. Has it rained? Itâ€™s been warning and threatening all week but just a dreary drizzle. Hereâ€™s a good one, came over the radio: Marriage is like soup. When the spooning is over itâ€™s cooled off. Hmm? Nope! Do please write and let me know how Minnesota gets along without a N.D. brat-like schoolmaâ€™am. And tell me how your grandma is, and the rest of you. Just in case youâ€™re interested, I miss you. Hope youâ€™re ok. Love, Marg"

column10: "Vining, Minn. June 10, 1945 Hi Marge, Itâ€™s eleven oâ€™clock Sun. eve, just got back after bringing Rubye to Henning. Looked for you there to you know, force of habit must be huh. I still think three months is a long time, thatâ€™s without seeing you of course. I love you Marge, thatâ€™s for sure. There was a wonderful sunset this eve. again, first one since last Sun. Makes me lonely you know. We all were down at Waltâ€™s this afternoon for Patsyâ€™s birthday, had a nice time. There were more people there than I could count. But then seven is my limit you know. Here I go, How are you Marge? Still disgustingly healthy I hope. Donâ€™t think you will have to go to Idaho do you huh, hope not, but then potatoes are all right I suppose or are theyâ€¦"

column10: "I still am milking all them cows you know, whitch gives me a lot of time to think, and thatâ€™s mostly of you of course, and boy what I donâ€™t think. Wish you were here kid, no fooling. If you think Iâ€™m nuts let me know, and Iâ€™ll still think the same, whitch makes me nuts, huh. Oh boy â€“ this is a tough job, this trying to write I mean. I think you better make two to my one ha, howâ€™s that? Hope you can read it anyhow. Thanks for trying if you canâ€™t. Nope [fate] now, need sleep you know. Write soon will you. Iâ€™ll be waiting. Might walk out there get you canâ€™t tell, if things get to bad. Night now. Love, Sam"

column10: "Page, N.D. June 15, 1945 Dear Sam, Before we go any farther, How are you? Iâ€™m fine too. Thanks heaps for working so hard writing me a letter! Iâ€™m sorry itâ€™s such a job, but why make it so? Practice, they say, makes anything easier, so keep a-practicing. Thanks too, for sending the fishing license. I havenâ€™t any idea of where or when Iâ€™ll use it around here. There just isnâ€™t any water except in our basement where I sit 3 times a day pumping it out. Oh, itâ€™s an electric motor so donâ€™t think of me sitting or standing hunched over, slaving away. Itâ€™s just that we have to watch the thing. You wouldnâ€™t think that Page was wet from the sign they have in one of the 2 eating places. The widow who runs it has a little 11 yr. old boy who gets tired of cleaning spittoons"

column10: "put up a sign, in large letters, on the counter: â€œIf you have to spit, spit out doors; Page needs the moisture.â€ The folks left at midnight last night for st. Paul to visit Hobart. Itâ€™s his treat for Fatherâ€™s Day & for their anniversary. One of the girls from Fargo came back with me this morning to keep me company until they come home on Monday. We had 3 nice days in Fargo but I was glad to get home again. Itâ€™s so peaceful here & I donâ€™t like mobs. The N.D. district meeting for all pastors was what we went for. Hazel (the friend from Fargo) & I have been taking turns at mowing the front lawn. Sheâ€™s hard at it now but oh my! The mower is so dull but Pap says â€˜itâ€™s the grass; the mower is ok.â€™ Try to convince a Norski against his will! How are the sunsets? So far I havenâ€™t seen one. Rain, when you going to come again, Rain!"

column10: "When you get tired of milking 18 cows and want a good long hike, come on out. Iâ€™d like to see you. No kidding. Next week one of our neighbors in Fargo & her little bot â€“ 7 yrs. â€“ are coming to stay several days & the Bible School teacher is coming here Sunday night for 2 wks. so weâ€™ll be kept a bit busy. Sometime in July I want to go to Bible Camp near Alexandria. The dentist & I have a date for July 6th. Happy thought. Right now itâ€™s lovely and sunny. Iâ€™d be in a mood for a ride & a sunset. Hm? The little girl next door is putting flowers in my hair â€“ Iâ€™m on the porch steps again. Itâ€™s supper time & what do we eat? Oh me, think itâ€™ll be soup. Itâ€™s so nice and so easy to fix. â€˜Bye for awhile, & do work hard again, if it means getting a letter. I like them. I do hope you know: I love you too. Marg P.S. Give that poor pooch or yours a pat from meâ€¦"

column10: "Vining, Minn. June 15, 1945 Dear Marg Me again, thatâ€™s kinda bad arenâ€™t it. But I canâ€™t help it you know. Got your letter and I thank you very much, it really helps thatâ€™s for sure. Was just looking at the calendar, thought it ought to be close to the first of Sept. pretty soon. But no luck. Boy thatâ€™s a long way off yet. Donâ€™t know if I can stand it. I did see Hans last Mon., and of course he told me he had talked to you out there. Heâ€™s talking Angus cattle worse than ever now. Wouldnâ€™t mind having a few of those $7500 ones though thatâ€™s for sure. Iâ€™d be around doing some talking in a hurry then, Iâ€™ll tell you. Olga has been staying with us for a few days. She just left this morning. Was fun having her around. Really helps having women in the house, thatâ€™s for sure. Dad hasnâ€™t been feeling so good for"

column10: "a while. Been in bed most of the time. Itâ€™s just the flu, but then thatâ€™s bad enough some times I guess. I wouldnâ€™t know of course. Grandmother and Chris (?) are about as usual. Grandma maybe feels a little better than she did for a while. Wouldnâ€™t be surprised if she lives to be a 100 yrs. Yet, only twelve to go, so why not, hm. We are having our league this Sun. eve instead of the fourth Sun. as there is a choir concert next Sun. eve. Will miss seeing you at league. Miss seeing you anyhow. You nut. How are you. Or maybe you will be there. Wish you would, thatâ€™s for sure. If those folks of yours ever heard of a guy by my name, you could tell em Hello, from me thatâ€™s if you feel like it of course. Nope gotta get(?) now itâ€™s midnight. Love, Sam"

column10: "Page, N.D. June 24, 1945 somewhere around 4 oâ€™clock Dear Sam, Another Sunday! And itâ€™s lovely. What are you doing; sleeping, or drowsing with thoughts a-whirling, or gadding and gabbing. Me â€“ Iâ€™m just sitting! For awhile I was on the lawn letting my legs get a bit of tan but theyâ€™re red instead and it got too too warm so I moved into the porch. Itâ€™s so quiet. Mom is sleeping and Mr. Olson came back from the country & went directly up to sleep so Iâ€™m here alone. One thing: itâ€™s nice & quiet, but Iâ€™d just as soon have a bit of noise. Sometimes itâ€™s lonesome the other way. This morning I substituted for a lady & taught a big class of 3rd graders. We had fun. Then services at which Mr. Olson spoke. Dad is in Ortonville for the day. Your letter came on Saturday and Iâ€™m glad it did. What do I think you are? Iâ€™ll tell you sometime. Remind me. Iâ€™m glad your Dad is up and around again. Whatâ€™s he painting now? So far, we havenâ€™t had rain for a week. It might decide to tonight. We are in a hail belt they tell us so I watch the sky. Say, did Ordean(?) come home & were he and Lorna married? No one ever said anything"

column10: "7 oâ€™clock! I went to sleep like the rest of the household. Now I fell much more like writing. Dad came home just a bit ago. He has been to a Golden Wedding too, on the way home from Ortonville. Itâ€™s still lovely outdoors and a wonderful evening for a lake ride. Boy! Wishful thinking, huh? The neighbors next door have been trying out the kidsâ€™ swing! The are both big people so I expected to hear a crack & a plop but none came. He farms with his son & he just said it was nice weather but even so the corn wonâ€™t be anywhere knee high by the 4th. Is that supposed to be a standard â€“ knee high by the 4th? Mr. Olson & I finished mowing & raking the lawn again last night. It was better this time, maybe because he sharpened the blades. He had a big blister on this thumb & I have 2, not on my thumbs but in the palms of my hands. Arenâ€™t we tough! Oh me, In time a few callouses should develop & protect me â€“ that is if Iâ€™m to keep up the yard which looks likely. There isnâ€™t a thing to write about Page or its surroundings or its people so maybe Iâ€™d better bring this epistle to an end. Of course I could write paragraphs on â€œstuffâ€ but Iâ€™ll wait. Bye, and do save a few pounds for winterâ€™s near. (?) Love, Margaret"

column10: "Page, N.D. June 19, 1945 Dear Sam, Itâ€™s a beautiful day! In fact it couldnâ€™t be nicer, except if you were here. We must be psychic or something. We both wrote on the same day and I got yours Monday & you must have gotten mine then too. Thanks so much for the last one. Did you have a nice time at League? I went to a Brotherhood program by my lonesome & helped introduce Mr. Olson, the Parochial School teacher to the few I know. Hazel went home Sunday aft. And the folks came Monday afternoon. Monday wasnâ€™t bad â€˜cause I cleaned this whale of a house but Sunday I was a bit moody. Monday we had 10 callers after the folks came, and Mrs. Meyer & Jimmy, our neighbors in Fargo, came back with Mom & Dad for 3 days. She kinda got lost"

column10: "in the mob of people & dishes. Today we washed â€“ clothes & hair. Tomorrow eve. we have a wedding here at home so weâ€™ll have fun decorating. There will probably be a baptism then too. Dadâ€™s going to Ortonville for Sat. & Sunday. Nora wrote today that Tilda(?) has a boy. Thatâ€™s nice. Nora is certainly kept busy though with 3 children around. She sounded as if it were fun nevertheless. Say, thatâ€™s too bad that your dad is sick. Is he better? I certainly hope so. Tell him to get well and stay that way till I see him again, at least. Itâ€™s nice you find it fine to have a woman around the house. Tell Olga she should come around oftener! Is she better? When does Rubye resign? Or doesnâ€™t she? None of my business. Yes, the folks have heard of you & thanks for the greeting. Iâ€™m not rushing things though. The spirea & flags are in full bloom so it looks nice around â€œhjar.â€"

column10: "This is about a Â½ hour later. Must be that I sit and dream and watch the clouds too much. I did write another page but it landed in the basket â€“ I couldnâ€™t make it out either. A storm was on the way and it looked like hail but now the sun is out again. Iâ€™m finding it awfully hard to write, so you arenâ€™t the only one, if you do yet. I love you and thank God continually for what you are to me, and pray that I may be worthy of you when and if you want me. I pray too that He may so direct us that we will grow in Him each day and learn to do His will in everything. Now Iâ€™d better go and mail this before I read it though again & it goes into a basket or some such. Iâ€™ve said what I truly wanted to say and found hard to say because Iâ€™m weak, I guess. Love, Marg"

column10: "Vining, Minn. June 21, 1945 Dear Marg, I just finished washing my hair and shaving off 4 days beard. Iâ€™m going to see Skilbred tonight you know. Wish I were. Thanks much for your last letter, you donâ€™t know how it makes me feel. Your praying that you would be worthy of me. After all who do you think I am. I pay to you know that through no fault of mine you should be sorry for tiring to me. Youâ€™re [you her] and everything outside of God to me, and may He grant that our love should last forever. Itâ€™s a beautiful night. The moon is hanging out over the lake. Would be perfect only if you were here. Things really are looking good now though even if the weather has been rather cool. Today was the first really warm day weâ€™ve had and the longest too of course. Makes the corn grow."

column10: "Dad was up and around again today, first time for a week. Feeling better because he was out with the paint brush today. Miss him a lot when he isnâ€™t around thatâ€™s for sure. You know this evening when I come up with the last oof themilk, there was a deer just below the house. You can guess who I thought of right then. It was a nice one to stood there for quire a while. So you really could have had a good look at that one. Iâ€™ll tie the next one that comes along, should I. Well not much nore happens around here outside of milking cows, feeding pigs, cultivating corn and things like that there. So I better sign off now. Should tell you that the mailbox by the side of the road means much to me, these days. So you better keep â€˜em coming. Sa for dwleve sa fra da. Love, Sam"

column10: "Vining, Minn. June 26, 1945 Dear Marg, How are you? Fine I hope. Me, I was never much better. Still weigh 111 lbs. anyhow thatâ€™s not so bad huh. Itâ€™s thundering, lightening and raining all at once. Nice day. Oh well first rain for over a week so we shouldnâ€™t bick[?]. Just cut the first hay today. So that will get nice and wet. Carl and I rented 50 acres of extra hay ground together. So we shouldnâ€™t run out of work for a while at least. The corn just might be knee high by the 4th is the weather stays right. This knee high business is just and old rule if it isntâ€™ that far ahead by the 4th then itâ€™s a poor chance it will be ripe corn unless we get a warm fall. Hi farmer. How you like all this farm stull. Better get used to it huh. Iâ€™m hoping you know."

column10: "Ordean didnâ€™t get his furlough. So Becker just simply went down there and married the guy. Lucky people. It happened kinda in a hurry. But then we hope they did the right thing anyhow. The Rev. Carlson of B.L. is having services in the hall at Vining every eve. this week. Rude and I were in this eve. Rubye had L.D.R. out at Bekkâ€™s[?], So we took her back to Henning afterward. She hasnâ€™t quit yet but she just might anytime I think. I hope anyhow. You said a while ago that you might go to Bible camp at Alex. You just wouldnâ€™t come by at that time would you. Wish you could. Iâ€™d really like to see you thatâ€™s for sure. Could be a few things Iâ€™l like to talk to you about you know. And then just seeing you again would help an awfull lot. Shorten the summer you know thatâ€™s been long enough already."

column10: "The congregation is planning a picnic for the 4th. Kind of a good idea I think. Keep people more were they belong donâ€™t you think. If I donâ€™t work Iâ€™ll be going there I suppose. Better come and join us. Then I wonâ€™t work for sure. Even if the hay gets wet. Walt say to tell you Hello sometime when I write. Iâ€™m sleepy again so better stop this scratching of mine. Be good. Love, Sam"

column10: "Page, N.D. June 28, 1945 Dear Sam, Here it is, the last Thursday in June. Canâ€™t believe it. And itâ€™s cold. It rained this morning but turned out to be a warm afternoon but tonight is definitely cool. We had entertainment this afternoon â€“ 2 little boys, one 10 & the other 7 yrs. old â€“ stayed with us while their father went to Fargo. They came home from Bible School and stayed until about 9:30 tonite. They were nice but I think I played Hide and Seek too long & walked up town too much. Iâ€™m tired. And appetites! Oh my! They did away with 6 sandwiches apiece after school & a pile of cookies. They ran it off though."

column10: "We tried out the new stove on a choc. Cake & some cookies. These farmers supply us with gobs of cream. Now we have 4 qts. on hand. All sour. Momâ€™s churning now. Nice farmers, what? Tomorrow we are invited to a coffee party in the county. 7 miles to drink coffee. Kinda dull but nice. Dad is stiff as a board tonight. He was in bed most of the day. His back is simply awful. Tomorrow heâ€™s to go to Ladies Aid in the country & then go on to Bible Camp â€“ 60 miles away. So heâ€™s better perk up. He has too much driving to do for his age â€“ and weight. This is the first day at home for him for over 2 wks. And sometimes I think I work!"

column10: "Of course you donâ€™t know what the word means either, do you? Yi! How long a day do you put in now? Thanks for the hello from Walt. Tell him â€œdittoâ€ from me. Toots left England on Fatherâ€™s Day for France to join an overseas unit for C-B-I theater. She & 3 other nurses were the first to be sent so she hopes sheâ€™ll be the first to come home too. Those who were sent to France went directly to China-Burma through the Med. Sea. No 30 day furlough en route nor nothing. She felt pretty blue when she wrote the news. We havenâ€™t heard since she left England Mother took the news well. A picnic for the congregation sounds like a good idea. How fun. And even if the hay gets wet take a day off to relax, huh?"

column10: "Havenâ€™t decided when Iâ€™ll go to Bible Camp but itâ€™ll be either the week of the 8th or the 15th of July. Do you suppose theyâ€™d let me on the bus in Fergus going through Vining? If they will Iâ€™ll let you know about it. Thanks for asking me. My eyes are practically shut. Iâ€™m in bed, loafing again. Itâ€™s nigh on to midnight though so Iâ€™d better call it quits. â€˜Night Love, Marg 1 P.S. Do you suppose Ordean considers himself lucky? Better find out. 2 P.S. Did I ever tell you that any time the gas & time situation (& the motor workings) permit, Page & its occupants â€“ at least 3 â€“ will welcome you? They will."

column10: "Vining, Minn. July 2, 1945 Dear Marg, Just me again. Little behind time maybe, But here just the same. We were at church yesterday morning. Then company in the afternoon. And last night we took Chris. Along to church. We didnâ€™t get back till 12 so I just went to bed without writing. Imagine Ruby really talked to Ken. about quiting So she will be home for a while at least. Iâ€™m going up to get her tomorrow night, luggage and all. She probably will take only a month or six weeks off. But thatâ€™s going to help her a lot, us too I think. So now you had better decide to come us for a few days after Bible Camp or something. I will see to that you will make some sort of bus connections. Even if I have to take you to Fargo. The old dodge still limps and chugs a little. But he will still get us there, I hope."

column10: "How about that Buick, have you driven it yet. Or maybe your Dad wonâ€™t take chances on it, hu. You really should learn though. Might come in handy. Of course I would still be glad to teach you, you know. Maybe the only thing I would know how to teach you. You shouldnâ€™t work so hard. Get old quick that way. Me, I just monky around16-18 hrs. a day so that arenâ€™t so bad for a little guy. We are going to that picnic the 4th. It will be down at Georgeâ€™s resort. You know that place? Hope we can have a softball game or something. Would give us some exercise. Might get stiff otherwise from laying around. I canâ€™t write. So hope I will see you soon. Then maybe I could try do some talking or wonâ€™t you let me. Night now. Love, Sam P.S. The day, well he would say Hello, if he knew enough. Heâ€™s not so brilliant thatâ€™s for sure."

column10: "Please: Excuse the pencil. Nothing else available. Mount Carmel Alexandria Tuesday Aft. Dear Sam, Itâ€™s nearly time for supper but if I donâ€™t start youâ€™ll never hear. We are really having a wonderful time and I do wish you could be here. Of course the nights are cold but the rain holds off so itâ€™s nice. We havenâ€™t tried the water yet. Last night we 3 slept together but Nora took the middle and baked all night & Rubye & I got cold so I moved out. Tonight we try it alone. The beds are good, the meals are good, the teachers are great, and everyone seems so nice. Could it be better? In the morning, classes begin at 9 and run till 12:15. In the afternoon you do as you please. Today we slept and went to a picnic at 4 oâ€™clock that was meant to be a get-acquainted affair."

column10: "One hour in the mornings is on the study of Acts & the topic: Every Christian a Witness for Christ. Itâ€™s a challenging topic and makes me feel how poorly Iâ€™ve done. The 2nd hour is on Joseph and his life. This morning it was on his hardships & why he had to have them. During the 3rd hour missionaries take turns telling of their experiences. This morning a lady from China, who has adopted a little Chinese girl, spoke, and the little girl sang Jesus Loves Me, in Chinese. She has black curly hair & is as cute as a bugâ€™s ear. We just got back from supper. Evening service is at 8:30 & lights are off at 11. So we have 2 hrs. of rest. Rest & more rest. Couldnâ€™t you use some? Some day I hope you can. I think Rubye & Nora are writing too."

column10: "Did you stay on the road Sunday Eve.? Not that youâ€™re a punk driver, but --. You know, someday Iâ€™ll have to quit my giggling and shall we talk? Or donâ€™t we need to? Of course Iâ€™m perfectly content to sit and listen to you, or just sit, as youâ€™ve noticed. I miss you, Sam and love you but I do so hope that both of us will put Christ first and grow in Him together. I know I do so many foolish things & say a lot more but over look them, will you, & Iâ€™ll keep trying. We learned a chorus last evening at the service and it made me think of our sunsets, & wished that more people could sing with us. Want to hear it? He give me joy in place of sorrows He gives me love that casts out fear. He gives me sunshine in place of shadows And beauty for ashes here. We donâ€™t know if we can stay for Sunday yet or not. They have a full camp for Sat. Weâ€™ll probably let you know when we decide to come. Thanks so very much for last weekend. Godâ€™s peace till I see you â€“ Yours, Marg"

column10: "Pencil again! July 12, 1945 7:10 P.M. Dear Sam, We are still having a wonderful time! Even with sunburn. We sun bathed this afternoon and now we look like pickled beets, but hope we wonâ€™t peel. The classes are more interesting all the time and Iâ€™m so thankful for being here. I needed some re-awakening. The food is excellent. Today we had large veal chops & mashed potatoes for dinner, and scrambled eggs for supper with lettuce and tomato salad. We eat and eat and Iâ€™m still hungry. This afternoon I had cookies & cake & 3 doughnuts & went to supper hungry. Must be the mountain air!"

column10: "They have room so that we can stay until Sunday aft. Are you planning to come down for the day? Or donâ€™t you know that Olga thought of a plan for doing that? If you do, weâ€™ll be happy to see you. If you donâ€™t, we will come to Fergus on the evening bus at 8:30. Thatâ€™s the 1st bus we can get out of here. Itâ€™s the same bus I came on from Millman[?], remember? If youâ€™d like to, or if the Dodge can make it, & you canâ€™t get here but can to Fergus, OK. If not weâ€™ll stay overnight & come out in the morning. You see, Iâ€™m still planning on a few days more in Minnesota. Marvin & Esther plan to come for Sunday & Nora will go with them."

column10: "So far I havenâ€™t learned to swim, and yesterday the white caps were so close & high that I tried floating & filled nose, eyes & ears so gave up. Want another job? Thatâ€™ll make 3 â€“ skating, driving, & swimming and Iâ€™ll never be efficient in any, maybe. And that isnâ€™t a dig at the teacher! Are tired of the scrawl? Just a few more lines. Youâ€™re busy & I shouldnâ€™t take your time, huh? You know, every time some one of the pastors says something especially interesting I wish you could hear it & every time I see something especially nice I wish you could too. Both happen quite often. Do you feel it?"

column10: "Another page â€“ oof! Last night 7 of us ate an 18-person watermelon!! We didnâ€™t feel too hot afterward. Was fun though. Now I gotta get ready for service. Nora & Rubye have a pile of company â€“ the walls vibrate. Bye till Sunday or Monday. Hope itâ€™s Sunday but donâ€™t worry if it isnâ€™t. Yours, Marg"

column10: "Page, N.D. July 25, 1945 Dear Sam, Yes, Iâ€™m home again. And N.D. is just as hot as Minnesota. I came on the train yesterday morning and the folks were on hand to meet. They must have been lonesome, huh? Rev. Tang and I missed the bus from Fergus to Fargo by about 10 minutes. Everetta didnâ€™t think they would pull out before we got in but they did, so Mr. Elliot took us to Detroit Lakes and we had 1Â¾ hr. wait there but got into Fargo 3 hrs. sooner than if weâ€™d waited in Fergus. But was it hot! We had to stand part of the way from D.L. to Fargo so it was a relief to really get out into a cool depot. I called mother and got a very welcome reception and an apology for the hurried and sharp call of last Thursday. She was in a hurry; must have been. Anyway, all was well. Then I went to spend the night with a friend who has a basement apartment. Smart of me to pick a cool place, donâ€™t you think? The train on Tues. morning was to leave at 7, but left at 8 so I didnâ€™t have to hurry."

column10: "We heard from Bernetta but she was still in France at that time, and expected to spend anywhere from 40-60 days on the water, so they must be going quite a way. Today we wash clothes. Yesterday we cleaned. Oh yes, I came home to find that we are moving again before Christmas! The parsonage is being remodeled finally so we move whenever it is ready. The crops are pretty fair, according to Pop. They begin harvesting next week. You should see the spuds & sweet corn in our back yard! They are really nice. The corn is nearly shoulder high. Must be the soil; canâ€™t be the gardeners. Are you still stacking hay? Yes, I know, thereâ€™s plenty of it. Have fun. Tell Carl hello, and Walt, if heâ€™s still with you. Thanks so much, Sam, for the nice time. It was a wonderful time, so nice that I might come again next year! Now I gotta go scrub clothes. Itâ€™s still mid-morning you see. â€˜Bye for now. The 4th of Sept. will come sooner than I think, maybe. Love, Margaret"

column10: "Page, N.D. July 26, 1945 Dearest Sam, This is more than you bargained for â€“ every day for 2 days! Youâ€™ll just have to stand it. I guess I spoke too soon about shoulder high corn. This morning we had an awful wind & rain storm which knocked most of the corn around here flat as pancakes. Now, at 6 p.m., we are having another terrific rain, with small hail. They were trying to hay around here too. It was plenty dry so guess we canâ€™t complain. Today we ironed and spacetained again. Every day since Monday weâ€™ve had visitors every afternoon. Sort of interrupts the day. What a rain â€“ straight down and white as a sheet. Still coming. Oh yes. I burned my knuckles while ironing. Just ran them along the side of iron while dreaming"

column10: "about something. Want to take a guess? Umhuh, I still think about what a nice time I had. Dad just asked what kind of cows you had and how many. Itâ€™s the only kind to have, I was told. Heâ€™d also like to milk about 7 of them. Wonder how the cows would behave. Poor things! Dad has a bad ankle and plugs out too soon lately. His foot is quite swollen. Mother has a very stiff neck & arm. Are they rheumatic or just getting old? We still laugh most of the day though. This morning they spent a couple of hours going over a box of Tootsâ€™ things which came the other morning."

column10: "What do you suppose will happen now that Churchill, too, is out of office. Itâ€™s too bad that both leaders had to go at such a crucial time but that was supposed to be for the best, what? Now Iâ€™d better go & get busy again. The rain is calming down. Dad says to tell you â€œhelloâ€ and Mom says she sends you her sympathy! Thatâ€™s what she thinks of me! I send you all of my love as usual, even when I forget to say so. You know, Iâ€™ve thanked God every day for quite some time that He sent you along my way. I ran across this prayer in a prayer book for Lutherans. Itâ€™s for one in Love â€“ could it be us? â€œO Lord, if it be Thy will, grant, I beseech Thee, that we two may understand each other better and better, and love each other sincerely. Above all, give us grace to keep"

column10: "I miss you much Marg. Got used to having you around a while you know. That was wonderful no fooling. Youâ€™re good for me thatâ€™s for sure. May He keep you as you are forever. Thanks for the greeting from your folks. You tell them both Hello from me too. Tell your Mom not to feel sorry for me. Because I know I am lucky. Will try have the car fixed this week. So I just might be out there sometime for about three hrs. or so. "

But I do get lonesome once in a while. Today was hot! Weâ€™re getting used to it though. I baked most of the day â€“ 2 cakes and bread. Yep, and even the bread was fit to eat. If people donâ€™t watch me Iâ€™m ok, I hope. Still willing to risk your, shall we say life, on it? Iâ€™ll taste everything first. Then if I fall over, you lay off. See? Yesterday was one long day. column10: "in Ayr and Buffalo so we didnâ€™t have any here. Mom and I slept late and then read and read and ate and wrote. Nice, lazy day. We had company in the evening. Oh yes, I told Mom and Dad I expected to leave them for a farm some day, and according to Mom, if Iâ€™m happy and content, thatâ€™s fine! Do you feel any better? I do. Get someone to milk those cows just once some day and fly out. Now, Iâ€™m a-talking through my hat again. Wishful thinking. Momâ€™s birthday is in a couple of weeks and I plan to ask 2 of her faithful friends, one from Wyndmere and one from Fargo, to come & spend the weekend with her."

column10: "I keep looking through the redeployment lists in the Fargo Forum every evening to see whether Howard Uhren is on his way home. So far, no! Say, the hail ruined whole sections around Buffalo last week. Most of Page county is all right. Even our corn is standing up again, and today Mom dug 2 small hills of potatoes and found 16 pretty good sized spuds. Dad was right proud."

So will be working there for a while now. We still didnâ€™t finish haying down on them meadows. So weâ€™ll have to do that after harvest. You will probably in on some of it yet. Be fun wouldnâ€™t it.
